Main characteristics: Good design must push technical boundaries and provide new solutions.
Do's: Utilize new technology to improve functionality; always seek new possibilities for expression.
Don'ts: Repeat existing forms without providing new utility; do not treat innovation as an end in itself.
Other characteristics: 10 principles of good design; Physical or digital products

Main characteristics: The primary purpose of a product is its utility (functional, psychological, and aesthetic).
Do's: Prioritize functional criteria and user utility; ensure design makes a product understandable and useful.
Don'ts: Ignore psychological or aesthetic utility; don't include features that do not serve the primary function.
Other characteristics: 10 principles of good design; User-centric utility

Main characteristics: Design clarifies the product's structure and makes it self-explanatory.
Do's: Make the product's function speak to the user; clarify the product's structure.
Don'ts: Rely on complex manuals for basic use; require high cognitive load for simple interactions.
Other characteristics: 10 principles of good design; Cognitive Load

Main characteristics: Does not make a product look more innovative, powerful, or valuable than it really is.
Do's: Be transparent and truthful about product capabilities and performance.
Don'ts: Manipulate the consumer with unfulfillable promises or false representations.

Main characteristics: Back to purity, back to simplicity; "Less, but better."
Do's: Concentrate on essential aspects only; strive for purity.
Don'ts: Burden the product with non-essentials or unnecessary complexity.

Main characteristics: The aesthetic quality of a product is integral to its utility and well-being.
Do's: Create well-executed, beautiful, and harmonious objects.
Don'ts: Assume beauty is secondary to function; treat design as just visual style without purpose.
Other characteristics: 10 principles of good design; Visual Design

Main characteristics: Avoids being fashionable and therefore never appears antiquated.
Do's: Design for longevity in both build and style; use high-quality materials.
Don'ts: Follow short-lived design trends or fads that lead to throwaway culture.

Main characteristics: Nothing must be arbitrary or left to chance; thoroughness and accuracy.
Do's: Ensure every detail is functional and precise; show care in the design process.
Don'ts: Leave loose ends or allow sloppy finishes in the final output.
Other characteristics: 10 principles of good design; Quality Control

Main characteristics: Design contributes to the preservation of the environment.
Do's: Minimize resource use and pollution throughout the product lifecycle.
Don'ts: Use materials that cannot be recycled or are toxic; design for a throwaway society.
Other characteristics: 10 principles of good design; Environmental Impact

Main characteristics: Products are tools, not decorative objects; design should be neutral and restrained.
Do's: Keep design discreet; leave room for user self-expression.
Don'ts: Over-design products to look like works of art; overwhelm users with loud elements.

Main characteristics: A class or module should have only one reason to change.
Do's: Keep modules focused on one function; encapsulate responsibility entirely.
Don'ts: Create 'God Objects' that handle multiple unrelated tasks.

Main characteristics: Depend upon abstractions, not concretions.
Do's: Use dependency injection; describe interfaces for low-level operations in business terms.
Don'ts: Hard-code dependencies between high-level and low-level modules.

Main characteristics: Entities should be open for extension, but closed for modification.
Do's: Use inheritance, interfaces, or composition to add behavior.
Don'ts: Modify core source code every time a new requirement arises.

Main characteristics: Objects should be replaceable with instances of their subtypes without breaking code.
Do's: Ensure derived classes honor the contract of the base class; extend base behavior safely.
Don'ts: Break expected behavior in subclasses; throw unexpected exceptions.

Main characteristics: Many client-specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface.
Do's: Split large interfaces into smaller, specific, and narrow ones.
Don'ts: Force clients to depend on methods they do not use (fat interfaces).

Main characteristics: The design is useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities.
Do's: Provide the same means of use for all users; make it appealing.
Don'ts: Segregate or stigmatize any users.

Principles: Universal Design: Simple and Intuitive Use
Main characteristics: Easy to understand regardless of user experience, knowledge, or language skills.
Do's: Eliminate unnecessary complexity; be consistent with user expectations.
Don'ts: Require high literacy or high cognitive load for basic navigation.

Main characteristics: Communicates necessary info effectively regardless of ambient conditions.
Do's: Use different modes (pictorial, verbal, tactile); ensure high contrast.
Don'ts: Rely on color or a single sense alone to convey critical meaning.

Main characteristics: Minimizes hazards and adverse consequences of accidental actions.
Do's: Provide warnings, fail-safe features, and easy undo options.
Don'ts: Design tasks that lead to irreversible or catastrophic errors if misclicked.

Main characteristics: Accommodates a wide range of individual preferences and abilities.
Do's: Provide choice in methods of use (e.g., right/left handed access).
Don'ts: Assume a single user speed or accuracy level; force a single interaction path.

Principles: Universal Design: Size and Space for Approach and Use
Main characteristics: Appropriate size and space provided regardless of body size or mobility.
Do's: Provide clear line of sight to elements; make reach comfortable for any user.
Don'ts: Obstruct the line of sight; ignore wheelchair access or reach ranges.

Main characteristics: Can be used efficiently and comfortably with minimum fatigue.
Do's: Allow user to maintain a neutral body position; minimize repetitive actions.
Don'ts: Require excessive force or sustained physical effort.

Main characteristics: The top-line vision and competitive advantage for long-term sustainability.
Do's: Align design goals with corporate KPIs; identify sustainable revenue streams.
Don'ts: Formulate plans without iterating; design in a vacuum ignoring business goals.
Other characteristics: 4 Tenets of UX Strategy; Product Management

Main characteristics: Simultaneous pursuit of differentiation and low cost to create a leap in value.
Do's: Solve problems in a way that makes competition irrelevant (Blue Oceans).
Don'ts: Compete purely on price in red oceans; add features just to copy others.

Main characteristics: Verifying assumptions with target users through direct feedback.
Do's: Use the Build-Measure-Learn loop; build MVPs to test market demand.
Don'ts: Assume you know what users want; design without direct user input.
Other characteristics: 4 Tenets of UX Strategy; Lean Startup

Main characteristics: Creating frictionless experiences that allow users to easily accomplish goals.
Do's: Collaborate with stakeholders at inception; focus on the key user flow.
Don'ts: Act like a "design hero"; clutter interfaces with features that obscure tasks.

Main characteristics: Making wise choices through evaluation and refining through cycles.
Do's: Use prototypes to fail fast; include stakeholders in evaluation.
Don'ts: Be paralyzed by indecision; use arbitrary standards for selection.
Other characteristics: 10 Attributes of Quality Design; Iterative Logic

Principles: Quality Performance: Problem Definition / Solution Validation
Main characteristics: Mapping the user problem against the validity of the proposed fix.
Do's: Clearly define the user problem before building; interview clients for needs.
Don'ts: Solve a problem that doesn't exist; move to implementation without validation.
Other characteristics: 10 Attributes of Quality Design; Design Process Pairing

Main characteristics: Internal/external interactions focused on collaboration and information sharing.
Do's: Foster open dialogue; use shared artifacts like blueprints or design systems.
Don'ts: Work in isolated silos; avoid documentation of project outcomes.
Other characteristics: 10 Attributes of Quality Design; Collaboration Dynamics

Principles: Quality Performance: Prior Knowledge / Divergent Thinking
Main characteristics: Idea generation based on research and creative exploration.
Do's: Apply existing principles; generate wide varieties of alternative solutions.
Don'ts: Lock onto the first idea found; ignore historical precedents or research.
Other characteristics: 10 Attributes of Quality Design; Cognitive Pairing

Principles: Quality Performance: Plan Execution / Professional Analysis
Main characteristics: Implementing design with technical rigor and evaluating its success.
Do's: Define tasks and milestones clearly; audit design-code alignment.
Don'ts: Release unfinished work; produce plans that are difficult for others to follow.
Other characteristics: 10 Attributes of Quality Design; Operational Pairing

Main characteristics: Viewing touchpoints as part of a broader connected ecosystem.
Do's: Map out dependencies between devices; design for stackability.
Don'ts: Focus on isolated interfaces; ignore the backend or connected sensors.
Other characteristics: 8 Design Tenets for Emerging Technologies; IoT / Robotics

Principles: Emerging Tech: Connecting People with Technology
Main characteristics: Using technology as a bridge/connective tissue between humans.
Do's: Humanize complex technical interactions; augment labor with technology.
Don'ts: Let tech become a barrier to social value; replace labor without considering meaning.
Other characteristics: 8 Design Tenets for Emerging Technologies; IoT / Robotics

Main characteristics: Systems and designers must adapt to new data, contexts, and domains.
Do's: Implement machine learning feedback loops; iterate based on system data.
Don'ts: Assume static environments; treat design as a "one and done" project.
Other characteristics: 8 Design Tenets for Emerging Technologies; IoT / Robotics

Principles: Emerging Tech: Working at a Variety of Scales
Main characteristics: Design must work from micro-interactions up to macro-systems/cities.
Do's: Ensure data integrity and consistency across all hardware and software nodes.
Don'ts: Optimize for one screen size only; ignore ergonomic constraints of small devices.
Other characteristics: 8 Design Tenets for Emerging Technologies; IoT / Robotics

Main characteristics: Finding the core of an idea to make it stick.
Do's: Strip away everything but the essential message; use analogies.
Don'ts: Bury the lead; overcomplicate the message with needless data.

Main characteristics: Getting people to act through narrative and mental simulation.
Do's: Use case studies; frame experiences as a hero's journey with a start and end.
Don'ts: Just list facts without context; present data without narrative context.

Main characteristics: Using sensory details and actionable info to lighten cognitive load.
Do's: Use specific, real-world examples and sensory language.
Don'ts: Rely on abstract concepts, vague jargon, or high-level abstractions.

Main characteristics: Building trust through authority, details, or statistics.
Do's: Use statistics carefully; provide 'vivid' details and social proof.
Don'ts: Make wild, unsubstantiated claims; use statistics without context.

Main characteristics: Making the audience care by appealing to self-interest or identity.
Do's: Give the device a personality; use human connections and humor.
Don'ts: Stay purely analytical, cold, or clinical.

Main characteristics: Grabbing attention by breaking patterns or defying expectations.
Do's: Use surprise to spark curiosity; include "Easter eggs" or happy surprises.
Don'ts: Be boring, overly predictable, or repetitive.

Main characteristics: Focus on foundational stability, reliability, and efficiency.
Do's: Ensure core features like booking work perfectly every time; prioritize speed.
Don'ts: Prioritize flashy/vanity features over system uptime and utility.
Other characteristics: Lyft Core Principles; Enterprise Design System

Main characteristics: Establishing trust through transparency and safety.
Do's: Provide clear feedback, transparent pricing, ETAs, and safety markers.
Don'ts: Hide critical data; use dark patterns; hide information that causes anxiety.
Other characteristics: Lyft Core Principles; Enterprise Design System

Main characteristics: Expressing brand identity and personality through design.
Do's: Use distinct colors, custom illustrations, and a friendly voice/tone.
Don'ts: Blindly copy competitor UI patterns; look exactly like every other utility app.
Other characteristics: Lyft Core Principles; Enterprise Design System

Main characteristics: Motion highlights hierarchy, relationships, and important interactions.
Do's: Show how new content relates to previous screens; direct attention.
Don'ts: Use motion without clear functional intent or for purely decorative reasons.

Main characteristics: Transitions are fluid, unbroken, and natural.
Do's: Coordinate simultaneous screen changes; ensure high frame rates.
Don'ts: Allow stuttering, lagging, or janky transitions that distract users.

Main characteristics: Movement should feel natural, expected, and mirror real-world physics.
Do's: Use ease-in and ease-out curves; base timing on user expectations.
Don'ts: Violate laws of spatial consistency; create movements that defy gravity.

Main characteristics: Motion must serve a purpose and provide feedback.
Do's: Use motion to guide user focus to new tasks and direct attention.
Don'ts: Add decorative animations that slow the user down or cause overload.

Main characteristics: Motion feels like it has weight, inertia, and friction.
Do's: Apply realistic physics and easing to transitions; relate to interaction velocity.
Don'ts: Use linear, robotic timing; use jerky or disconnected transitions.

Main characteristics: Elements should maintain flow and spatial context between states.
Do's: Use shared element transitions; maintain orientation during navigation.
Don'ts: Use abrupt cuts or jarring transitions that disorient the user.

Main characteristics: Motion relates to the user's point of origin and environment.
Do's: Animate elements from their touch/click point; adjust for viewport size.
Don'ts: Trigger animations from random locations; use 'one-size-fits-all' motion.

Main characteristics: Engaging the customer as a partner in the design process.
Do's: Use personas and participatory design to involve the user.
Don'ts: Rely solely on rigid initial requirements or contract negotiation.
